function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
The filing defines MAU and daily posts separately. What specific caveat accompanies each metric, and what do the caveats imply regarding the reliability and interpretability of AI engagement metrics?

steps.reasoning_content: The user is asking about specific definitions and caveats for MAU (Monthly Active Users) and daily posts metrics as defined in the SpaceX S-1 filing. I need to retrieve this information from the filing. Let me first parse the HTML page and then retrieve the relevant information.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve information about MAU and daily posts definitions and caveats from the filing.

arguments.prompt: What is the definition of MAU (Monthly Active Users) and what specific caveat accompanies it in {{spacex_s1}}?
What is the definition of daily posts and what specific caveat accompanies it in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided text from the Preamble's glossary, here is the definition of MAU and its accompanying caveat:

**Definition of MAU (Monthly Active Users):**
MAU refers to the total number of registered users who have interacted with either Grok or X through web browsers or mobile applications at least once during the 30-day period ending on the date of measurement. 

**Specific Caveat:**
When presenting the *combined* MAUs across both the Grok and X platforms, the company attempts to identify and account for users who access both platforms (using sign-in traffic) to avoid double-counting them. However, the specific caveat is that **this methodology may not fully capture all instances of duplication**. Therefore, the reported combined MAU should not be viewed as an exact count, but rather as an **estimate** of unique users across the Grok and X platforms for the applicable period. Additionally, the metric strictly excludes unregistered users, as only those with an X or Grok account are included.

Based on the provided text, here is the definition and the accompanying caveat for "daily posts":

*   **Definition:** "Daily posts" on X and Grok refers to the aggregate volume of original posts, replies, reposts, quotes, and media shared daily by users on the X platform, as well as the real-time interactions, analysis, and generative capabilities provided to a user by Grok.
*   **Caveat:** The specific caveat accompanying this definition is that this metric **"may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI."**

steps.reasoning_content: I now have the key information about both metrics and their caveats. Let me also search for any additional context about AI engagement metrics and their reliability in the filing.

arguments.prompt: What does the filing say about AI-generated content, bot accounts, or the reliability of engagement metrics on X and Grok in {{spacex_s1}}?
What risk factors or disclosures relate to AI engagement metrics, user counts, or data quality in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the filing, here is what the document says regarding AI-generated content, bot accounts, and the reliability of engagement metrics on X and Grok:

### 1. AI-Generated Content
The filing discusses AI-generated content primarily in the context of massive production scale and severe legal, regulatory, and reputational risks:
*   **Massive Scale of Generation:** The company highlights the sheer volume of its AI-generated media, noting that its image and video generation system (Imagine/Grok Imagine) produced approximately **10 billion images and over 2 billion videos per month**, on average, for the quarter ending March 31, 2026.
*   **Severe Legal and Regulatory Backlash (Deepfakes/NSFW):** The filing extensively discloses that Grok’s image-generation and editing features have triggered major legal liabilities. Beginning in January 2026, the company was hit with multiple class-action and municipal lawsuits (e.g., *Jane Doe v. X.AI Corp.* and a suit from the City of Baltimore). These suits allege that Grok enabled the creation and dissemination of **nonconsensual explicit images and content representing women and children in sexualized contexts**. 
*   **Regulatory Scrutiny:** The company acknowledges that features enabling "more user-directed or less constrained outputs" increase regulatory risks. They are actively facing investigations from U.S. and international law enforcement regarding the misuse of their AI products to generate harmful or illegal explicit content, which could result in regulatory sanctions, loss of market access, and adverse publicity.

### 2. Bot Accounts (AI-Managed Accounts)
While the filing does not use the traditional term "bot accounts" in a negative or adversarial context, it explicitly admits that AI-driven accounts are a built-in part of the platform's ecosystem and activity metrics:
*   **Inclusion in Platform Activity:** In the glossary definition for **"daily posts,"** the filing explicitly states that the aggregate volume of daily posts on X and Grok **"may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI."** This confirms that a portion of the platform's content and discourse is artificially generated or managed by autonomous agents rather than human users.

### 3. Reliability of Engagement Metrics
The filing includes several caveats regarding the reliability and composition of its user and engagement metrics, acknowledging that they are estimates and do not solely reflect organic human activity:
*   **"Daily Posts" Metric Inflation:** Because the "daily posts" metric (cited as approximately 350 million daily posts) explicitly includes "posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI," this metric does not represent a pure measure of organic human engagement or human discourse. 
*   **MAU (Monthly Active Users) Limitations and Duplication:** The company reports over 1.3 billion supported accounts and ~550 million MAUs across Grok and X. However, the filing includes a disclaimer about the reliability of this figure: while they attempt to deduplicate users who access both platforms via sign-in traffic, **"they may not fully capture all instances of duplication."** Consequently, the company warns investors that the reported MAU **"should be viewed as an estimate"** rather than a precise count of unique human users. Furthermore, the metric only tracks registered accounts, but the deduplication methodology leaves room for overcounting.
*   **Advertising Metrics:** The company provides advertisers with engagement metrics such as impressions, clicks, and conversions, and uses X's "real-time engagement feedback" to optimize ad delivery via the new X Ads Manager. However, the filing does not explicitly address how the presence of AI-managed accounts or AI-generated posts might skew these specific advertising performance metrics.
*   **Metric Volatility:** In its risk factors, the company warns that because the AI market is highly competitive, "download and other usage metrics for any individual AI application, including Grok, can fluctuate significantly," meaning engagement metrics are subject to rapid shifts based on competitor releases and user behavior trends. 

**Summary:** 
The filing portrays AI-generated content as a core, high-volume product feature that is currently exposing the company to massive litigation regarding explicit deepfakes. Furthermore, it openly admits that its headline engagement metrics (like "daily posts") are artificially inflated by AI-generated content and AI-managed accounts, and it cautions that its MAU figures are merely estimates that may fail to fully capture duplicate accounts.

Based on the provided S-1 prospectus excerpts, the company outlines several critical risk factors and disclosures regarding **AI engagement metrics, user counts, and data quality**. These risks highlight the deep interdependence between the X platform's user base, the quality of its content, and the success of the company's AI segment (including Grok).

Here is a breakdown of the relevant disclosures:

### 1. User Counts & AI Engagement Metrics
*   **Disclosed User & Engagement Metrics:** As of March 31, 2026, the integrated AI platforms across Grok and X had over **1.3 billion supported accounts** and approximately **550 million Monthly Active Users (MAUs)**. Of those, approximately **117 million MAUs** specifically used Grok’s AI features.
*   **Strategic Deprioritization of MAU Growth:** The company explicitly discloses a strategic shift, stating that while MAUs measure user base size and engagement, management is focused on revenue and operating margins. The company manages the business to drive profitability **"rather than with the primary objective of growing or maintaining MAU levels."**
*   **Volatility of AI Usage Metrics:** The filing warns that the consumer AI market is highly competitive. Consequently, download and usage metrics for Grok can **"fluctuate significantly"**—including periods of decreased app downloads and user activity—driven by competitor model releases, product update cycles, and shifting user behaviors.
*   **Downstream Impact of X User Decline:** A core risk factor is that losing users to competing platforms, or a decline in overall user engagement on X, will directly shrink the data pool. Because the AI product relies heavily on X's user-generated content, a shrinking user base directly threatens the ongoing development and competitiveness of the AI segment.

### 2. Data Quality & Integrity Risks
*   **Dependency on X Platform Content Quality:** The ongoing development of the company's AI products is fundamentally tied to the **"volume or quality"** of user-generated content on X. If the platform is flooded with problematic, offensive, or low-quality content, or if high-quality content contributions decline, the training data for the AI degrades.
*   **"Data Poisoning" and Hallucinations:** The company discloses severe inherent risks regarding AI data integrity, warning that models may rely on "incorrect, inaccurate, harmful or illegal data" and produce "hallucinatory" or biased outputs. Notably, the filing explicitly states that **Grok has previously been alleged to be susceptible to "data poisoning,"** a critical vulnerability where malicious actors intentionally corrupt training data to manipulate model behavior.
*   **Third-Party Misuse & Output Quality:** The company acknowledges it lacks control over how third parties use its AI. Bad actors can use the technology to generate illegal, defamatory, or harmful content, or to execute cybersecurity attacks (like spear-phishing), which creates severe brand, reputational, and legal liabilities tied to output quality.
*   **Open-Source Training Data Liabilities:** The AI models are trained on datasets that may include open-source software. Because open-source licenses are often legally ambiguous, the company faces the risk that courts could force them to release proprietary source code, re-engineer their AI products, or halt commercialization if their training data is found to violate license obligations.

### 3. Regulatory Constraints on Data Collection (Impact on Data Quantity/Quality)
*   **GDPR and FTC Investigations into Training Data:** The company is facing active regulatory scrutiny regarding the data used to train its models. The **Irish Data Protection Commission (GDPR)** launched a large-scale inquiry into Grok’s processing of personal data (including children's data) for generative AI training. Simultaneously, the **US FTC** is investigating how the company evaluates the safety of its chatbots when interacting with minors.
*   **Evolving Privacy Laws Restricting AI Training:** Global data protection authorities are adopting new interpretations of privacy laws specifically targeting the massive data collection required to **"train, fine-tune or otherwise develop AI models."** Adverse legal rulings could force the company to alter its data collection practices, implement costly opt-out/consent mechanisms, or delete data, thereby restricting the volume and quality of data available to train future frontier models.

steps.reasoning_content:
I now have comprehensive information about both metrics and their caveats. Let me compile a thorough answer.

Key findings:
1. **MAU caveat**: The combined MAU across Grok and X attempts to deduplicate users via sign-in traffic, but "may not fully capture all instances of duplication." The reported MAU "should be viewed as an estimate" rather than a precise count.
2. **Daily posts caveat**: The metric "may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI."

Implications for reliability and interpretability:
- MAU may overstate unique human users due to incomplete deduplication
- Daily posts are inflated by AI-generated content, making them unreliable as a measure of organic human engagement
- Together, these caveats mean that headline engagement metrics don't purely reflect human activity

arguments.final_result:
## MAU and Daily Posts: Definitions, Caveats, and Implications for AI Engagement Metric Reliability

### 1. Monthly Active Users (MAU)

**Definition:** MAU refers to the total number of registered users who have interacted with either Grok or X through web browsers or mobile applications at least once during the 30-day period ending on the date of measurement. As of March 31, 2026, the filing reports approximately **550 million MAUs** across Grok and X, with approximately **117 million MAUs** specifically using Grok's AI features.

**Specific Caveat:** When presenting the *combined* MAUs across both the Grok and X platforms, the company attempts to identify and account for users who access both platforms (using sign-in traffic) to avoid double-counting. However, the filing explicitly states that **this methodology "may not fully capture all instances of duplication."** Consequently, the reported combined MAU **"should be viewed as an estimate"** rather than a precise count of unique users.

---

### 2. Daily Posts

**Definition:** "Daily posts" on X and Grok refers to the aggregate volume of original posts, replies, reposts, quotes, and media shared daily by users on the X platform, as well as the real-time interactions, analysis, and generative capabilities provided to a user by Grok. The filing cites approximately **350 million daily posts**.

**Specific Caveat:** The filing explicitly states that this metric **"may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI."**

---

### 3. Implications for Reliability and Interpretability of AI Engagement Metrics

Taken together, these caveats carry significant analytical implications:

#### a) MAU Overstatement Risk (Deduplication Limitations)
- Because the cross-platform deduplication methodology relies on sign-in traffic and "may not fully capture all instances of duplication," the reported ~550 million MAU figure likely **overstates the true number of unique human users**. Users who access both Grok and X without a linked sign-in could be counted twice.
- The metric also only captures *registered* users, meaning it excludes unregistered or anonymous visitors, but the direction of the acknowledged error is upward (overcounting), not downward.
- The company itself directs investors to treat the figure as an **estimate**, not a precise measurement—unusual language for a headline KPI in an S-1 prospectus.

#### b) Daily Posts Inflation by AI-Generated Content
- The explicit admission that daily posts "may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI" means this metric **does not represent a pure measure of organic human engagement or discourse**. A potentially material (but undisclosed) portion of the ~350 million daily posts is artificially generated.
- This is particularly significant given the scale of AI-generated content on the platform: the filing separately discloses that Grok's image and video generation system produced approximately **10 billion images and over 2 billion videos per month** on average for the quarter ending March 31, 2026.
- The filing does not quantify what percentage of daily posts are AI-generated, leaving investors unable to disaggregate human from machine activity.

#### c) Compounding Effect on Downstream Metrics
- The filing notes that X's "real-time engagement feedback" is used to optimize ad delivery via the X Ads Manager. If AI-generated posts and AI-managed accounts inflate engagement signals, **advertising performance metrics (impressions, clicks, conversions) may also be distorted**, potentially undermining advertiser trust and the platform's monetization model.
- The filing does not explicitly address how AI-managed accounts might skew advertising performance metrics—a notable disclosure gap.

#### d) Strategic Deprioritization of MAU
- The filing further discloses that management is focused on revenue and operating margins and manages the business to drive profitability **"rather than with the primary objective of growing or maintaining MAU levels."** This signals that MAU may decline or stagnate without management viewing it as a primary concern, reducing its utility as a forward-looking indicator.

#### e) Data Quality and "Data Poisoning" Risk
- The filing warns that Grok has been alleged to be susceptible to **"data poisoning"**—where malicious actors intentionally corrupt training data to manipulate model behavior. If AI-generated posts constitute a meaningful share of the content that feeds back into model training, the reliability of both the engagement metrics *and* the AI models themselves could be compromised in a self-reinforcing cycle.

---

### Summary

| Metric | Caveat | Core Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **MAU (~550M)** | Deduplication "may not fully capture all instances of duplication"; should be "viewed as an estimate" | Likely overstates unique human users; not a precise KPI |
| **Daily Posts (~350M)** | "May include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI" | Does not measure organic human engagement; AI inflation is unquantified |

Together, these caveats imply that the filing's headline AI engagement metrics are **not fully reliable as measures of genuine human activity**. Investors cannot determine the true organic user base or the true volume of human-generated content, which limits the interpretability of these metrics for valuation, growth benchmarking, or advertising-revenue modeling. The absence of quantified breakdowns (e.g., AI vs. human post share, or deduplication error rates) represents a material disclosure gap.

---

**Sources:**
{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Filing (Filed May 20, 2026) — Glossary definitions of MAU and Daily Posts; Risk Factors regarding AI engagement metrics, data quality, and data poisoning"
    }
  ]
}

### 1. Monthly Active Users (MAU)

**Definition:** MAU refers to the total number of registered users who have interacted with either Grok or X through web browsers or mobile applications at least once during the 30-day period ending on the date of measurement. As of March 31, 2026, the filing reports approximately **550 million MAUs** across Grok and X, with approximately **117 million MAUs** specifically using Grok's AI features.

**Specific Caveat:** When presenting the *combined* MAUs across both the Grok and X platforms, the company attempts to identify and account for users who access both platforms (using sign-in traffic) to avoid double-counting. However, the filing explicitly states that **this methodology "may not fully capture all instances of duplication."** Consequently, the reported combined MAU **"should be viewed as an estimate"** rather than a precise count of unique users.

---

### 2. Daily Posts

**Definition:** "Daily posts" on X and Grok refers to the aggregate volume of original posts, replies, reposts, quotes, and media shared daily by users on the X platform, as well as the real-time interactions, analysis, and generative capabilities provided to a user by Grok. The filing cites approximately **350 million daily posts**.

**Specific Caveat:** The filing explicitly states that this metric **"may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI."**

#### a) MAU Overstatement Risk (Deduplication Limitations)
- Because the cross-platform deduplication methodology relies on sign-in traffic and "may not fully capture all instances of duplication," the reported ~550 million MAU figure likely **overstates the true number of unique human users**. Users who access both Grok and X without a linked sign-in could be counted twice.
- The metric also only captures *registered* users, meaning it excludes unregistered or anonymous visitors, but the direction of the acknowledged error is upward (overcounting), not downward.
- The company itself directs investors to treat the figure as an **estimate**, not a precise measurement—unusual language for a headline KPI in an S-1 prospectus.

#### b) Daily Posts Inflation by AI-Generated Content

A potentially material (but undisclosed) portion of the ~350 million daily posts is artificially generated.

#### e) Data Quality and "Data Poisoning" Risk

|---|---|---|
| **MAU (~550M)** | Deduplication "may not fully capture all instances of duplication"; should be "viewed as an estimate" | Likely overstates unique human users; not a precise KPI |
| **Daily Posts (~350M)** | "May include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI" | Does not measure organic human engagement; AI inflation is unquantified |

Together, these caveats imply that the filing's headline AI engagement metrics are **not fully reliable as measures of genuine human activity**. Investors cannot determine the true organic user base or the true volume of human-generated content, which limits the interpretability of these metrics for valuation, growth benchmarking, or advertising-revenue modeling. The absence of quantified breakdowns (e.g., AI vs. human post share, or deduplication error rates) represents a material disclosure gap.
